title: Navigating the Currents: Sri Lanka’s National Security Debate in the New Indian Ocean Order
gist: A teaser excerpt arguing that Sri Lanka, sitting 10 nautical miles from major Indian Ocean sea lanes, must balance Chinese infrastructure investment against US and Indian security partnerships while a reformist new president struggles against entrenched institutional pressures.

insights.claim: Sri Lanka sits just 10 nautical miles from major Indian Ocean sea lanes carrying a substantial portion of global energy and container traffic, positioning it as both a vital maritime actor and a focal point of strategic contestation.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: Sri Lanka's proximity to critical energy/container lanes makes it a chokepoint-adjacent asset in US-China maritime competition, relevant to shipping and supply-chain risk.
insights.quote: just 10 nautical miles from major sea lanes carrying a substantial portion of global energy and container traffic

insights.claim: Intensifying US-China competition, via Beijing's infrastructure investments versus Washington's maritime-domain-awareness and freedom-of-navigation partnerships, is forcing Sri Lanka to balance economic opportunity against security calculations.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: Sri Lanka is a live test case of whether Chinese infrastructure money outcompetes US security ties in the Indian Ocean, a template for other small littoral states.
insights.quote: manifested most visibly through Beijing’s infrastructure investments and Washington’s partnerships on maritime domain awareness and freedom of navigation

insights.claim: India's neighbourhood doctrine increasingly influences Colombo's decisions on maritime security and defence cooperation.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: India is a third pole shaping Sri Lankan alignment, complicating any simple US-vs-China framing of Indian Ocean competition.
insights.quote: India’s neighbourhood doctrine has also increasingly influenced Colombo’s decisions on maritime security and defence cooperation

insights.claim: Sri Lanka's 2026 national security debate hinges on whether the country can break from the institutional pressures that constrained previous reform attempts before internal and external conditions foreclose the option.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: A closing window for institutional reform is the key variable; failure entrenches fiscal fragility and strategic drift.
insights.quote: whether the country can break from the institutional pressures that have constrained previous reform attempts, and whether it can do so before both the internal and external environments foreclose the option
insights.locator: Sri Lanka's national security debate in 2026

insights.claim: The September 2024 election of President Anura Kumara Dissanayake was the most critical political rupture in Sri Lanka's postwar history, carrying a mandate for clean governance and economic recovery that has proven harder to deliver over time.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: Gap between the AKD mandate and delivery signals continued political/fiscal uncertainty, relevant to Sri Lanka sovereign-risk and reform trajectory.
insights.quote: the most critical political rupture in the island’s postwar history
insights.locator: The election of President Anura Kumara Dissanayake

insights.claim: Sri Lanka's national security is compounded by non-traditional threats including IUU fishing, drug trafficking, transnational crime, and climate-induced disasters, alongside fiscal fragility from the 2022 Aragalaya movement.
insights.speaker: CNAS
insights.so_what: Layered non-traditional threats plus post-Aragalaya fiscal fragility broaden the risk surface beyond great-power competition.
insights.quote: such as IUU (Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated) fishing, drug trafficking, transnational crime, and climate-induced disasters
